name: awesome-agv
description: Comprehensive sets of standards and practices designed to elevate the capabilities of AI coding agents.

commit.message:
refactor(agent): revamp Flutter Riverpod 3 standards and resolve audit findings

Flutter / Riverpod 3 Updates:
- Comprehensively updated Riverpod 3 standards for consistency
- Replaced legacy Riverpod 2 patterns with deterministic Riverpod 3 patterns
- Ensured structure and rules are optimized for AI agent follow-ability

Awesome-AGV Consistency Audit Fixes:
- Align Go examples with canonical backend structure
- Standardize test co-location language overrides
- Fix broken cross-references (@avoid-circular-dependencies)
- Clarify E2E paths for monorepo vs single-app
- Standardize em-dash formatting in Related Principles
- Add missing rule mandates to priority hierarchy
fix(docs): add frontmatter to html graph for github pages
docs(rules): add interactive and static rule dependency graphs

docs(rules): generalize Rust workspace example, add mock.rs, add scope note

- project-structure-rust-cargo.md: replace Pathfinder-specific crate names
  with generic myapp-* names so the workspace template is project-agnostic;
  add mock.rs to single binary crate task/ feature for parity with Go layout
  and architectural-pattern.md testability requirements
- rust-idioms-and-patterns.md: add > Scope: blockquote after Core Philosophy
  for structural parity with go-, typescript-, flutter-, and vue-idioms files

docs(core): update rules count, add idiom files, and detail opinionated stack

This commit brings the documentation up to date with the recent rule system audit and restructuring:

- Fixed stale rule counts across all docs (30 -> 38 rules)
- Removed all references to the absorbed 'avoid-circular-dependencies' rule
- Documented the 5 new language-specific idiom and pattern files
- Documented the 4 language-specific project structure files
- Added 'Opinionated Technology Choices' section to README and index
- Added a framework change guide to adapting.md for customizing the stack
- Fixed hardcoded '30 Rules' string in the CLI installer

refactor(rules): audit and restructure agent rule system for integrity

- Fix cross-references across 28 rule files to use absolute paths
- Deduplicate content between mandate and principles files
- Extract language-specific idioms into dedicated files
- Absorb avoid-circular-dependencies into code-organization-principles
- Rename rust-idioms-and-safety to rust-idioms-and-patterns for consistency
- Add language idiom files: go, typescript, vue, flutter, rust
- Update code-completion-mandate to delegate tooling to idiom files
- Add rule reference validation script (scripts/validate-rule-refs.sh)
- Update debugging-protocol rust reference to new filename
feat(workflow): enforce integration and E2E testing gates in workflows

refactor(rules): modularize project structure and refine debugging protocols

- Split project-structure.md into language-specific rule files (Flutter, Go, Rust, Vue).
- Added rust-idioms-and-safety.md.
- Updated debugging-protocol skill to improve logic.
Update README.md

docs: propagate report naming fix to GitHub Pages and add modularity note

- Update audit report naming to {HHmm} in skills.md, workflows.md,
  best-practices.md
- Add modularity callout to getting-started.md and README.md:
  rules/skills work independently of workflows

fix(cli): align banner box and add quick-start hint

- Rewrite printBanner() with computed padding to fix box skew
- Add quick-start section showing /orchestrator, /audit, /quick-fix
- Copy root README into cli/ at publish time for npm package page
- Append {HHmm} timestamp to audit/debug report filenames to
  prevent same-day naming collisions
fix(ci): remove allow-same-version to enforce version bumps
fix(ci): allow same-version in npm publish workflow

refactor: rename project from antigravity-setup to awesome-agv

- Update all references in README, CLI, and GitHub Pages
- Consolidate CLI documentation into main README
- Implement dynamic versioning from git tags in CI
- Bump Node.js requirement to >=20.0.0 (LTS)
- Remove duplicate cli/README.md

feat(cli): add awesome-agv CLI tool for 1-click installation

- Add npx awesome-agv CLI that fetches latest .agent/ from GitHub
- Zero runtime dependencies, uses Node.js built-ins only
- Interactive overwrite prompt with --force flag
- Add GitHub Action for npm publishing with provenance
- Update README with npx command as primary install method
- Mark CLI roadmap item as complete
update readme, configure github pages
overhaul workflow, add skills, improve rules
add workflows
refactor architecture & test strategy, create project structure and completion mandate
update readme
Create LICENSE

releases.name: v1.1.2
releases.body:
### What's New & Fixed

**🚀 Major Revamps**
Flutter / Riverpod 3 Redesign:
- Comprehensively replaced all legacy Riverpod 2 patterns with modern, deterministic Riverpod 3 patterns.
- Structure, idioms, and rules fully optimized to ensure AI agents can follow and implement Flutter patterns seamlessly.

**🧹 Consistency Audit Resolutions**
Conducted a deep consistency audit across all 38 rule files, 10 workflow files, and 7 skill directories, successfully applying several key consistency fixes:
- Go Backend Structure Alignment: Aligned all Go examples (logic.go, storage.go, etc.) and internal paths with the canonical project structure.
- Universal Test Co-location Rule: Added universal language overrides to test co-location rules (e.g., properly deferring to Flutter's test/ directory convention and Rust's inline #[cfg(test)] blocks).
- E2E Path Clarifications: Standardized and clarified test folder structures for Monorepo (apps/e2e/) versus Single-App (e2e/) projects.
- Cross-reference Integrity: Fixed broken internal cross-references (such as the formerly missing @avoid-circular-dependencies.md) to guarantee AI agents never drop context due to broken links.
- Typography & Formatting Standardization: Standardized em-dash (—) formatting in all Related Principles sections across the framework.
- Rule Hierarchy Additions: Added missing core mandates (like the Concurrency and Threading Mandate) into the formal rule-priority.md hierarchy.

**Full Changelog**: https://github.com/irahardianto/awesome-agv/compare/v1.1.1...v1.1.2

releases.name: v1.1.1
releases.body:
**Overview**
Patch release that polishes the Rust rule files for generality and structural parity with the other language-specific idiom and project structure files.

**Changes**
- Rust Project Structure (project-structure-rust-cargo.md)
  - Generalized workspace example: Replaced Pathfinder-specific crate names (pathfinder-*) with portable myapp-* names, preventing the agent from cargo-culting project-specific naming into unrelated Rust workspaces.
  - Added mock.rs to the single binary crate task/ feature layout — aligns with Go layout (storage_mock.go) and architectural-pattern.md testability requirement (production adapter + test adapter per port).
- Rust Idioms (rust-idioms-and-patterns.md)
  - Added > Scope: note after Core Philosophy for structural parity with go-idioms-and-patterns.md, typescript-idioms-and-patterns.md, flutter-idioms-and-patterns.md, and vue-idioms-and-patterns.md.

releases.name: v1.1.0
releases.body:
**Features**
Workflows: Enforced integration and E2E testing gates in workflows.

**Rule System Integrity & Refactoring**
- Comprehensive Audit: Audited and restructured the agent rule system for enhanced integrity. Fixed cross-references across 28 rule files to use absolute paths, ensuring zero conflicts and high cohesion.
- Language-Specific Idioms: Added dedicated files for `go`, `typescript`, `vue`, `flutter`, and `rust` idioms. Updated the code completion mandate to delegate specific linting and formatting tooling to these newly established idiom files.
- Project Structure Modularization: Split the general `project-structure.md` into language-specific rules (`project-structure-flutter-mobile.md`, `project-structure-go-backend.md`, `project-structure-rust-cargo.md`, `project-structure-vue-frontend.md`).
- Rule Consolidation: Deduplicated content between mandate and principles files, and absorbed circular dependency guidance into `code-organization-principles.md`.
- Debugging Protocol: Refined debugging protocols and updated Rust language references to use the correct pattern files.
- Validation Script: Introduced `scripts/validate-rule-refs.sh` to prevent broken references in the future.

**Full Changelog**: https://github.com/irahardianto/awesome-agv/compare/v1.0.4...v1.1.0

releases.name: v1.0.4
releases.body:
Minor addition to CLI install success message. 
Update documentation in readme and github pages.

**Full Changelog**: https://github.com/irahardianto/awesome-agv/compare/v1.0.3...v1.0.4

releases.name: v1.0.3
releases.body:
### 🔧 CLI Improvements
Fixed banner box alignment — The version box displayed after npx awesome-agv no longer has skewed borders or inconsistent letter spacing. Character widths are now computed correctly regardless of ANSI color codes.  

Added quick-start hint — After installation, the CLI now shows three slash commands to get started immediately:  
`💡 Quick start — open a chat with your agent and try:`  
*   `/orchestrator   Build a feature end-to-end`  
*   `/audit          Review code quality`  
*   `/quick-fix      Fix a bug fast`

### 📦 NPM Package
README now appears on npmjs.com — The root repository README is automatically copied into the package at publish time, so the [npm package page](https://www.npmjs.com/package/awesome-agv) displays full documentation instead of a blank page.

### 📝 Workflow & Skill Fixes

Audit report naming collision fix — Reports now include {HHmm} timestamps (e.g., review-findings-auth-2026-02-16-1430.md) to prevent overwriting when running multiple audits of the same feature in a single day. Updated in:  
  * /audit workflow  
  * Code Review skill  
  * Debugging Protocol skill

---

Full Changelog: [https://github.com/irahardianto/awesome-agv/compare/v1.0.0...v1.0.3](https://github.com/irahardianto/awesome-agv/compare/v1.0.0...v1.0.3)

readme_html:
About Awesome AGV

Awesome AGV provides a comprehensive sets of standards and practices designed to elevate the capabilities of AI coding agents. It provides a suite of strict rules distilled from software engineering best practices that ensure generated code is secure, defensible, and maintainable. It also provides specialized skills that will help throughout software development.

Instead of just generating code that works, the rules and skills ensures agents generate code that survives.

⚠️ Opinionated by design. Awesome AGV ships with opinionated defaults for specific technology stacks. See Opinionated Technology Choices for details and how to customize.

While this configuration is originally designed for Antigravity, it is built on standard markdown-based context protocols that are easily portable to other AI coding tools. As a matter of fact, the original form Technical Constitution was first created for Gemini CLI

You can drop this configuration into the context or custom rule settings of:

  • Roo Code
  • Claude Code
  • Any other agentic tool that supports custom system prompts or context loading.

For example, the principles of the Rugged Software Constitution which is based on Rugged Software Manifesto are universal and will improve the output of any LLM-based coding assistant.

Key Features

  • 📏 38 Rules — covering security, reliability, architecture, maintainability, language idioms, and DevOps.
  • 🛠️ 7 Skills — specialized capabilities for debugging, design, code review, and more.
  • 🔄 10 Workflows — end-to-end development processes from research to ship.
  • 🏗️ Two-Tier Rule System — always-on mandates + contextual principles for zero-noise enforcement.

💡 Everything is modular. Rules and skills work independently — you don't need workflows to benefit from them. Use only what you need, modify anything, or build your own workflows. It's a toolkit, not a framework.

Getting Started

To equip your AI agent with these superpowers, follow these steps.

Prerequisites

  • An AI Coding Assistant (Antigravity, Roo Code, Cline, etc.)
  • A project where you want to enforce high standards.

Installation

Quick Install (recommended):

npx awesome-agv

This downloads and installs the latest .agent/ directory into your current project. Your AI agent will automatically pick it up — no additional configuration needed.

Options:

Flag Description
[target-dir] Directory to install into (default: ./)
--force, -f Overwrite existing .agent/ without prompting
--help, -h Show help

Examples

# Install into current directory
npx awesome-agv

# Install into a specific project
npx awesome-agv ./my-project

# Overwrite existing installation without prompting
npx awesome-agv --force

Manual Install:

  1. Clone this repository or copy the .agent folder into the root of your project. 
    cp -r /path/to/awesome-agv/.agent ./your-project-root/
  2. Ensure your AI agent is configured to read from the .agent directory (most of well-known AI coding assistant are adhering to the .agent convention by default, no action needed) or manually ingest the .agent/rules/** as part of its system prompt.

Usage

Once installed, the rules and skills in this repository become active for your agent.

Rule Architecture

The setup uses a two-tier rule system to minimize noise while maximizing coverage:

Type Trigger Purpose
Mandates always_on Non-negotiable constraints loaded in every session (security, logging, code completion).
Principles model_decision Contextual guidance activated only when working on relevant areas (e.g., database rules activate only when writing queries).

Conflicts between rules are resolved by Rule Priority — security always wins.

Rule Dependencies

The rules are highly interconnected to provide comprehensive coverage. You can explore these relationships using the Interactive Rule Dependency Graph, or view the static diagram below.

Comprehensive Rule Suite

The power of the setup comes from its extensive collection of rules covering every aspect of software engineering.

🛡️ Security & Integrity

  • Rugged Software Constitution: The core philosophy of defensible coding.
  • Security Mandate: Non-negotiable security requirements.
  • Security Principles: Best practices for secure design.

⚡ Reliability & Performance

  • Error Handling Principles: Techniques for robust error management.
  • Concurrency & Threading: Safe parallel execution and deadlock prevention.
  • Concurrency & Threading Mandate: When to use (and not use) concurrency.
  • Performance Optimization: Writing efficient and scalable code.
  • Resource Management: Handling memory and system resources responsibly.
  • Monitoring & Alerting: Health checks, metrics, and graceful degradation.
  • Configuration Management: Environment variables, secrets, and config hierarchy.

🏗️ Architecture & Design

  • Core Design Principles: Fundamental software design rules (SOLID, DRY, etc.).
  • API Design Principles: Creating clean, intuitive, and versionable APIs.
  • Architectural Pattern: Testability-first design with I/O isolation.
  • Project Structure: Feature-based organization (the single source of truth for layout).
  • Project Structure — Go Backend: Go-specific directory layout.
  • Project Structure — Vue Frontend: Vue/React frontend layout.
  • Project Structure — Flutter Mobile: Flutter/RN mobile app layout.
  • Project Structure — Rust/Cargo: Rust workspace and crate layout.
  • Database Design: Schema design, migrations, and query safety.
  • Data Serialization: Safe data handling and formats.
  • Command Execution: Principles for running system commands securely.

🧩 Maintainability & Quality

  • Code Organization: Structuring projects for readability.
  • Code Idioms: Following language-specific best practices.
  • Go Idioms: Go-specific patterns, error handling, concurrency, and tooling.
  • TypeScript Idioms: TypeScript type system, strict mode, async patterns.
  • Vue Idioms: Vue 3 Composition API, Pinia stores, composables.
  • Flutter Idioms: Flutter/Dart, Riverpod state management, freezed models.
  • Rust Idioms: Ownership, error handling, async with tokio, clippy.
  • Testing Strategy: Ensuring code is verifiable and tested.
  • Dependency Management: Managing external libraries safely.
  • Documentation Principles: Writing clear and helpful documentation.
  • Logging & Observability: Ensuring system visibility.
  • Logging & Observability Mandate: All operations must be logged — no exceptions.
  • Accessibility Principles: WCAG 2.1 AA compliance for UIs.
  • Git Workflow: Conventional commits, branch naming, and PR hygiene.

🔄 DevOps & Operations

  • CI/CD Principles: Pipeline design, Docker, and GitHub Actions.
  • Code Completion Mandate: Automated quality checks before every delivery.
  • Rule Priority: Conflict resolution when rules contradict each other.

Specialized Skills

  • Debugging Protocol: Systematic approach to solving errors.
  • Frontend Design: Guidelines for creating visually appealing UIs, based on Anthropic Frontend-Design Skills
  • Mobile Design: Production-grade mobile interfaces for Flutter and React Native.
  • Sequential Thinking: A tool for breaking down complex problems, an adaptation from Sequential Thinking MCP Server
  • Code Review: Structured code review protocol against the full rule set.
  • Guardrails: Pre-flight checklist and post-implementation self-review.
  • ADR (Architecture Decision Records): Document significant architectural decisions with context and trade-offs.

Development Workflows

The setup includes opinionated, end-to-end workflows that chain rules and skills into structured development processes.

🏭 Feature Workflow (/orchestrator)

The primary workflow for building features. Phases execute sequentially — no skipping.

Research → Implement (TDD) → Integrate → E2E (conditional) → Verify → Ship
Phase Workflow Purpose
1. Research /1-research Understand context, search docs, create ADRs, uses Qurio default to web search
2. Implement /2-implement TDD cycle: Red → Green → Refactor
3. Integrate /3-integrate Integration tests with Testcontainers
3.5. E2E /e2e-test End-to-end validation with Playwright
4. Verify /4-verify Full lint, test, and build validation
5. Ship /5-commit Git commit with conventional format

🔧 Specialized Workflows

Workflow When to Use
/quick-fix Bug fixes with known root cause (<50 lines)
/refactor Safely restructure code while preserving behavior
/audit Code review and quality inspection (no new features)

Roadmap

  • Include more specialized skills to aid development process (7 skills shipped).
  • Add development workflows for structured feature delivery (10 workflows shipped).
  • Add language-specific idiom and pattern rules (Go, TypeScript, Vue, Flutter, Rust).
  • Create a CLI tool for easier installation (npx awesome-agv).
  • Add automated validation scripts to check if an agent is following the constitution.
  • Publish comprehensive documentation site (GitHub Pages).

Opinionated Technology Choices

Awesome AGV ships with opinionated defaults for specific technology stacks. Each stack has dedicated idiom files with patterns, tooling, and verification commands.

Stack Default Choice Idiom File(s)
Backend Go — vanilla stdlib, minimal deps go-idioms-and-patterns.md
Frontend TypeScript + Vue 3 — Composition API, Pinia, Vitest typescript-idioms-and-patterns.md, vue-idioms-and-patterns.md
Mobile Flutter + Riverpod — freezed models, go_router flutter-idioms-and-patterns.md
Systems Rust — tokio, thiserror/anyhow, clippy pedantic rust-idioms-and-patterns.md

Using a different framework? The idiom files are modular — swap or edit them to match your stack. See the Adapting guide for which files to change.

Project Adaptation Guide

This setup supports different project structures:

Project Type Adaptation
Monorepo (default) Use as-is
Single backend Remove frontend rules/workflows, keep backend paths
Single frontend Remove backend rules/workflows, keep frontend paths
Microservices Adapt project-structure.md per service, add service mesh rules
Mobile (Flutter/RN) Adapt frontend rules, add mobile-specific accessibility/testing

To adapt: Edit project-structure.md, the relevant idiom file, and 4-verify.md to match your project layout.

Contributing

Contributions are what make the open source community such an amazing place to learn, inspire, and create. Any contributions you make are greatly appreciated.

  1. Fork the Project
  2. Create your Feature Branch (git checkout -b feature/AmazingFeature)
  3. Commit your Changes (git commit -m 'Add some AmazingFeature')
  4. Push to the Branch (git push origin feature/AmazingFeature)
  5. Open a Pull Request
